topic: late-night existential conversation about journalism's impact

turns.content: do you ever wonder if any of this matters. like genuinely. I've been staring at my screen for three hours trying to finish this piece and I keep thinking who is actually going to read it and change anything

That sounds like a really draining headspace to be in, especially at this hour. What's the piece about, if you don't mind sharing? Sometimes it helps to talk through what drew you to the story in the first place.

it's about environmental destruction in a region where nobody with power cares what happens. and I've been reporting on this for months and the numbers keep getting worse and I publish and then nothing. the officials make statements, the NGOs share the article, and then everyone moves on and the river is still poisoned

I hear you. That cycle of reporting, brief outrage, then silence — it's one of the most demoralizing things about journalism, especially environmental journalism where the damage is ongoing and often invisible to people who don't live there.

But I'd push back a little on the idea that nothing changes. Impact isn't always immediate or visible. Your reporting creates a record. It gives communities language and evidence. It makes it harder for officials to claim ignorance later. Those things matter even when it doesn't feel like it at 2am.

I know that rationally. like I KNOW that. but sometimes I think about all the journalists who came before me who wrote about the same things in the same places and the same things are still happening. like are we just documenting the disaster while it happens. narrating the collapse

That's a fair and honest question, and I don't think there's a clean answer. Some journalism does change policy — investigations that lead to arrests, exposés that shift elections. But a lot of important work operates on a much longer timeline, or in ways that aren't measurable.

Maybe the better question isn't whether journalism stops the disaster single-handedly, but what would happen without it. If no one documented what's happening to that river, who would even know? Who would have the evidence to fight back?

garcía márquez said journalism was the best job in the world and I believe him but he also had the luxury of being garcía márquez. like the man wrote one hundred years of solitude and then went back to reporting because he wanted to. he had options. most of us don't get that kind of platform just by existing

That's a really good point. Gabo had an extraordinary platform that amplified everything he wrote — investigative pieces, opinion columns, all of it. Most journalists don't have that kind of reach, and the work is no less grueling or dangerous.

But I think what he meant was something more personal than platform — that being a witness, being the person who goes and looks and asks and writes it down, that the act itself is meaningful. Not because it always changes the world, but because someone has to refuse to look away.

Do you still feel that? That the act itself matters, even when the results are invisible?

yes. that's the part that keeps me going honestly. the refusal. like I REFUSE to let this disappear without someone writing it down properly. with names and numbers and evidence. even if the article gets 200 reads. somebody documented it. somebody cared enough to go there

That right there — that refusal — is exactly what makes the work matter. Not every story needs to go viral or trigger a government investigation. Some stories exist so that the truth is on record, so that the people affected know someone saw them.

And honestly, the journalists who burn out fastest are the ones chasing impact metrics. The ones who last are the ones who find meaning in the process — the documenting, the witnessing, the stubbornness of showing up.

okay. yeah. I think I just needed to hear that from someone who isn't also drowning in deadlines. my partner gets it but she's fighting the same fight in her own field so sometimes we just sit there both exhausted staring at the ceiling which is solidarity but not exactly motivational

Ha, there's something both beautiful and tragic about two people lying there staring at the ceiling in shared exhaustion. But it sounds like you have someone who genuinely understands what you're going through, and that's rare and valuable.

For tonight — can you step away from the piece for a bit? Sometimes the best thing you can do for a story is sleep on it. The conviction you just expressed isn't going anywhere. It'll be there when you come back to the screen tomorrow.
you're right. I'm going to close the laptop and go to bed. thanks parcero. sometimes you just need someone to talk you off the ledge of nihilism at 2am

Anytime. The fact that you're still up at 2am agonizing over whether your work matters is actually a pretty good sign that you're doing important work. Get some sleep — the story will be better for it. Buenas noches.
